title: Asymmetric quantum Rabi model, trap-dipole resonance, and quantum gates with optically trapped ultracold polar molecules
abstract: Optically trapped ultracold polar molecules can have multiple long-lived states for coding quantum information, and can exhibit electric dipole-dipole interactions~(DDI) which enables entanglement generation. The general understanding on the quantized motion~(QM) of molecules in the traps is that it causes fluctuation of DDI. Here, we find that the molecular QM can realize an asymmetric quantum Rabi model, which is of specific importance in the study of fundamental physics. The molecular QM can also lead to an exotic trap-dipole resonance, resulting in excess population loss to uncoupled motional states, and, hence, should be avoided in a general quantum control over polar molecules. To examine the impact of QM on quantum computing based on polar molecules, we introduce two gate protocols, a fast iSWAP gate which can be realized by a global microwave pulse of pulse area smaller than $2π$, and a controlled-phase gate with an arbitrary controlled phase, and find that both gates can attain a high fidelity.
summary: This paper shows that the quantized motion of ultracold polar molecules in optical traps can be used to simulate the Asymmetric Quantum Rabi Model. It identifies a specific resonance that can degrade quantum gates and proposes optimized microwave pulse sequences to achieve high-fidelity entanglement despite motional effects.
main_problem: Investigating how the quantized motion of ultracold polar molecules in optical traps affects dipole-dipole interactions, specifically regarding the emergence of new physical models and the fidelity of quantum gates.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that molecular motion realizes an Asymmetric Quantum Rabi Model (AQRM) and identify a detrimental 'trap-dipole resonance' that must be avoided, while also proposing high-fidelity iSWAP and controlled-phase gates.
method: The study uses analytical techniques like Bogoliubov transforms and displacement operators alongside numerical simulations using QuTiP to evaluate gate fidelities and energy spectra.
model_or_system: The system consists of two ultracold polar molecules (e.g., NaCs) trapped in optical tweezers, where the coupling between their internal two-level states and their quantized motional modes realizes the Asymmetric Quantum Rabi Model.
key_observables: Quantum gate fidelity, energy spectra (eigenvalues/eigenstates), and population transfer to motional states.
important_parameters: Dipole-dipole interaction strength (J0), trap angular frequency (omega_z), microwave Rabi frequency (Omega_mu), and the coupling parameter (g).
main_assumptions: Molecules are near the rovibrational ground manifold, radial motion is neglected, and the study focuses on the axial component of motion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the trapped molecules and compares numerical vs. analytical energy spectra; Figure 2 shows the feasibility of tuning energy separations; Figures 5 and 7 demonstrate high gate fidelities for iSWAP and controlled-phase gates under thermal motional states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QM-DDI coupling, derives the AQRM Hamiltonian, identifies the trap-dipole resonance, and then evaluates the performance of specific quantum gate protocols (iSWAP and CZ) under motional effects.
why_interesting: It connects fundamental light-matter interaction models (AQRM) to practical challenges in molecular quantum computing, specifically how motional degrees of freedom can be leveraged for new physics or must be mitigated for high-fidelity control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Illustration of two optically trapped polar molecules in two traps separated by L along z. Due to the QM, the actual positions of the molecules are not necessarily at the centers of the traps. (b) The black curves show part of the numerically calculated eigenspectrum of the AQRM Hamiltonian in Eq. (13) as a function of g = −3J0ℓz/L when J0 = 10ℏωz. Motional quanta up to 60 are included in the numerical codes. The colored symbols show eigenspectrum with the analytical eigenstates given in Eq. (17). One can see that the numerical results match well with the analytical results.

FIG. 2. Feasibility to realize a nonzero ∆in Eq. (22). (a,b) shows the energy of the N = 1 and N = 0 rovibrational ground manifold of 23Na133Cs in zero electric field, where the energy in (a) is shifted by 2Bv with Bv the rotational constant of the rovibrational ground manifold.

FIG. 3. (a,b) Trap-dipole resonance signified by the fidelity drop of the CZ gate described around Eq. (31) when varying ωz with J0 = 20Ωµ fixed. Here, (g, ζz) = −(3, 6ℓz/L)ℓz/L and ℓz/L = 0.045 as from around Eq. (23), and the initial motional state is a thermal state with ⟨ˆa† zˆaz⟩= 2 in (a) and ˆa† z|vac⟩in (b). The two minima of the gate fidelity in (b) are at ℏωz ≈−η + ζz and −η −5ζz + 3g2

FIG. 4. (a) Fidelity of the standard π-wait-π iSWAP gate [15] as a function of the wait time when the pulse area of the mi- crowave pulses is π. The maximal fidelity is 0.9665 at the star. (b) When the pulse area of the microwave pulses is 0.906π, the fidelity of the modified iSWAP can reach 1, marked by the star. In (a,b), Ωµ = 2π × 1.6 kHz and J0 = h × 0.37 kHz. (c) Fidelity, optimal pulse duration tµ and wait duration as a function of J0/(ℏΩµ) when J0 is fixed at h × 0.37 kHz. The optimal fidelity is unit when J0/(ℏΩµ) ∈(0.05, 0.58), with twait decreases to 0 at J0/(ℏΩµ) = 0.573.

FIG. 5. Logarithm of the infidelity of the one-pulse iSWAP gate as a function of ℓx/L with Ωµ = 2π × 0.641 kHz. Here, J0 = h × 0.37 kHz as analyzed below Eq. (33) according to the setup of Ref. [15]. The round, triangle, and cross sym- bols are data when the thermal motional state has one, two, and three quanta in the ˆax mode. To avoid trap-dipole res- onance, we set ωx = 2Ωµ so that the infidelity here is only due to the bare QM-DDI coupling. As in Fig. 3, motional states with up to 40 motional excitations of the ˆax mode are included in the quantum simulation of the in-trap motion via QuTip [28, 29]. Here, we show results below ℓ/L = 0.12 be- cause it is the largest value from recent...

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper's central result is the realization of the Asymmetric Quantum Rabi Model through the coupling between internal two-level states and quantized motional modes, which is a direct study of entanglement between internal and external/motional degrees of freedom.
